title: Early-life exposure to hardship increased risk tolerance and entrepreneurship in adulthood with gender differences.
ehr_evidence: No EHR data mentioned; the study uses historical and demographic data related to China's Great Famine.
summary: The study investigates the impact of early-life hardship (specifically China's Great Famine) on adult risk tolerance and entrepreneurship, exploring gender differences and behavioral mechanisms.
key_findings: Individuals who experienced more hardship were more likely to become entrepreneurs.
Hardship was associated with greater risk tolerance in both men and women.
Hardship increased business ownership only among men.
The increase in entrepreneurship was at least partly due to conditioning rather than selection.
captured_features: famine intensity (proxied by population loss from weather shocks)
population: Adults who experienced China's Great Famine (1959-1961)
limitations: Potential confounding by social norms regarding gender roles
